# Research brief

## main idea
Create a video game: an open-ended turn-based rpg where there is no pre-written story. Instead, it is a world simulator where the player is one of the characters in that world. all of the characters in the world are controlled by llms, and the world itself is controlled by an orchestrator agent. the idea is that the world is a completely open-ended simulation where anything can happen. the orchestrator should do its best to set up interesting narratives through interplay between the characters and other things it creates, but ultimately whether or not those things will happen is unknown. the multi-agent simulation must unfold to see. the vibe of this game is cute fantasy.

## game design principles
* be open-ended: there is no hard-coded story, and there is no intended way to accomplish the game. by only implementing the mechanics of the world, we should ensure that every playthrough will be completely different, and the game will be flexible enough to allow the player to use creativity to solve tasks.
* complexity from simple rules: in this game, we only implement basic, atomic actions for characters, and we only seed characters with backstories. all of the complexity of the game emerges through the multi-agent interactions between characters and the world.
* no preferential treatment: as much as possible, the non-player characters should follow the same implementation interface as the player. that is, they have an inventory with some weapons/armor, and some actions available to them. the main difference should be that their decision function is an LLM, while the player is controlled by a human. this should not only help implementation, but also make multiplayer balanced.
* emergent strategy: a beautiful game is one where simple rules beget complex dynamics, and interesting high-level strategies. furthermore, there should not be one winning strategy—winning approaches should be non-transitive like rock paper scissors, where one approach counters an opposing approach but is weak to another approach. we should endeavour to create this dynamic as much as possible, like in chess: the movements of the pieces are simple, yet there is infinite complexity in strategies.
* progression: this is a delicate balance. 
    * many games fall into the pitfall of having progression towards an end goal or a failure to keep creating challenges: once the player becomes too powerful or achieves the main goal, they lose interest. in pokemon, once you defeat the league, the only goal left is to complete the pokedex, which is a somewhat indirect goal—it is just stated that it should be done, but there is no problem directed at the player that incentivises them to do that (the league does present such a problem, where the player loses if they are not strong enough, but then once they beat the league they are done). similarly, in a game where you acquire better and better weapons and armour and skills, there comes a point where the game becomes too easy because it’s no longer a challenge (and of course it’s not fair to directly scale enemy strength with the player because then they are not rewarded for progressing). 
    * the opposite problem is a game where there is zero progression. this would be something like garry’s mod or minecraft creative mode, where you can do anything, and therefore there is no goal driving the player to improve.
* make a headless mode (i.e. be able to run the game without the visuals). this will make it many times easier to debug the game if it’s not working.

## gameplay / mechanics
* for the lower-level goals, the user should be sent on quests. since the quests will be entirely handled by the characters in the game and specific to the scenario, we have no direct control over this. instead, this has to be done by tweaking the prompt of the orchestrator and npc character agents. the “quests” should not be random or forced—they should make sense in the context of the situation for the given npc, and it needs to make sense to the npc as well (why would they want to offer this). of course, in most cases, it wont just be an npc offering a quest, it should be something more organic: an adventure that unfolds as a wild goose chase as the player goes around collecting information.
* to solve the progression problem mentioned earlier, i recommend this as the overarching goal: territory conquering. this offers scalable progression, as the player feels accomplished by conquering a region of the map, but the world is procedurally generated and infinite, so they can keep going. also, since the world is dynamic and npcs follow their own agenda, ownership of the territory is dynamic. it’s possible that another npc can “capture back” a territory. its also not story-specific, as it can be adapted to any situation by being flexible with the definition of “conquering”.
    * in practice, i think the best way to calculate ownerships is through a distance-weighted average of weighted sentiment. that is, at each point,  we look at all of the entities in some large radius. we assign a weight to each of the entities based on their distance from the point, with closer being a higher weight (e.g. with a softmin), and do a weighted sum between that and their weighted sentiment towards each player they know. if the highest ownership score is above some threshold, then the associated player will gain ownership of that point. if not, then the region remains neutral.
    * “weighted sentiment” itself is calculated by weighting the raw sentiment of an npc towards another character (player or other npc) by that npc’s status and level (i.e. battle strength). status is an attribute on a character’s character sheet, determined by an llm (for npcs, it can be updated by the orchestrator agent, but generally it will stay static). for the player, the status is equal to the level. the idea here is that people with higher status represent diplomatic threats and people with higher level represent military threats.
    * raw sentiment is a variable maintained in each character’s character sheet. it keeps track of the sentiment of that character towards every other character that they know of.
    * Reach goal: i think it makes sense to take this a step further, and do a step of diffusion: the total raw sentiment can be calculated with the raw sentiment of a character towards the player plus [the raw sentiment of the character towards each of the people they know times their raw sentiment towards the player], where that second term is normalised over all targets (i.e. if you sum the diffused term over all targets it equals 1). the idea here is that if you have high sentiment with the village leader, and the villagers all have a high sentiment with the village leader, then the villagers should have some diffused sentiment towards you as well.
    * doing quests for someone will raise their sentiment (the amount determined by the llm based on the nature of the quest). it is also theoretically possible to raise the sentiment of a character towards someone just by talking to them, but it should be difficult so the game can’t be cheated by just talking to everyone instead of playing. only truly novel diplomacy should even be considered for a sentiment improvement.
    * just like other actions in this game, the game decides whether or not to improve the sentiment with a target based on an ability check against a difficulty class + WIS + max(status, level) (or something like that, i’m not sure what the actual calculation should be). if the CHA ability score plus the roll is higher, then the action succeeds. 
    * also just like other actions, if an ability check is performed to potentially raise the sentiment, any subsequent similar attempts should not trigger an ability check. to make sure of this, the character should store in their memory the context that triggered the ability check.
* the difficulty should scale as a function of the distance from the player’s starting point. the difficulty determines the level of the enemies that spawn. this creates a dynamic where the player has to work harder and harder to conquer areas further away, introducing an interesting progression.
* the game will be real-time until combat starts (someone attacks someone else), and then the entire local area will switch to turn-based mode, just like baldurs gate.

## characters
the characters themselves have stats sheets with ability scores that affects what they are good at in the game, a backstory, current goal, and personality, an inventory with items that they can use/trade, and their own persistent memory that is continuously updated by adding things that are noteworthy (quests, relationships, events they witness, etc), and a recent history of dialogues. the system also keeps track of last n actions and state transitions (state deltas are converted to readable text like moved 1m north), which the characters can see. the characters can also observe the environment (test if it’s better to use a text-based representation in sparse vs occupancy grid format, or if it’s better to provide an image of what they can see). each of the characters also has a list of high-level actions that the llm controlling it can call:
* go to (position, item, or character)
* jump (position. possibly use this to mount animals/vehicles too)
* attack (target character, item attacking with)
* say (text, target character or just shout, in which case characters in the vicinity hear it)
* propose/accept/deny trade (items/money in and out. this handles “giving” too, which is a trade with nothing in return)
* pick up / drop item (to/from ground or chest)
* examine (get info on item or person in sight range. not all info, just observable info)
* interact (target entity, item used to interact. generic interaction command - example: lockpicking)
* wait (time)
* etc
of course, many of these commands have the ability to fail (e.g. if they try to jump farther than their stat sheet says they can). in those cases, the action should return a message why it failed. the agents control loops should run on some frequency while they are taking an action (in case they want to change their mind, but the bias should be towards continuing the action), and immediately when the last action finishes or something interrupts it (e.g. they are attacked while moving to a position).

## orchestrator
the orchestrator agent is primarily in charge of:
* spawning new characters with unique personas/skills/items/backstories that it predicts will weave together to create interesting narratives
    * when new characters are spawned, one good way to aid in personality generation is by first randomly sampling their skills sheet. their skills in each stat are sampled from ranges, based on the unit role (created by the llm) and the local region difficulty. if, for example, they roll a really high charisma but a low wisdom, then the LLM can use this as a starting point to write an interesting personality and backstory.
* resolving world events through tool use, such as adding/removing objects, editing properties and states (e.g. moving an object), playing animations, etc. for example, if the character steps on a pressure plate that activates a trap, the orchestrator is in charge of instituting the effects of that trap.
* Reach goal: adding/editing/removing persistent rules. Often, an entity or character or the world will need to obtain a persistent effect (for example, the user places an object enchanted to emit cries for help, luring nearby entities that fail some check—this requires a rule, as it is unsustainable to run the llm every time step to resolve a persistent effect )
* Reach goal: terrain generation guidance. the orchestrator should be able to tweak the parameters of terrain generation (or regenerate in that region with new parameters) if it is necessary for the story.

## skills, actions, and items
* when the player levels up, they get to add an additional point to one of their skills (strength, constitution, charisma, dexterity, wisdom, intelligence). this proficiency will be used in several places:
    * in ability checks, which occur both when interacting with characters or entities and when trying to perform some action.
    * in weapon attack rolls, which determine if the attack hits. the calculation will take into account the enemy’s armour class, as well as the user’s relevant ability score(s) relative to the weapon’s level. for example, if the weapon is a high level and requires INT for the attack rolls, then if the user’s INT ability score is low, there is a very low probability of hitting. similarly, for armour, if the armour is level 8 and requires WIS, then the armour class will not be at the item’s max potential unless the user’s WIS is at level 8. 
* there are no discrete classes in the game, and no spells / special actions or skills / passive abilities are “learned” by the characters themselves. instead, they are attached to items. for example, there might be a staff that emits the “fireball” spell. or there might be a pendant that allows the user to disappear into a shadow in one place and emerge in another shadow. some weapons might have multiple types of attacks, or an attack that has multiple effects.
* unlike DnD which has spell slots, this game will have “cooldowns”. every attack on an item will have an associated cooldown time, which represents the number of turns before that attack can be used again. more powerful attacks will have larger cooldown times.
* melee attacks, spells, projectile attacks, and actions should all share a base effect class that can be customised with different effects, damages, properties, and animations. for example, a melee attack is a type of attack with a certain range and hitbox shape, and an arrow is just an attack with a different sprite, animation, hitbox shape and movement, etc. spells can also be customised using the same procedure. these effects can be composed in novel ways: magic missile is a projectile that has a split effect and a homing effect. under this paradigm, it should be possible to randomise weapons. weapons with higher rarity (common, uncommon, rare, legendary, mythic, eternal) have a higher chance of doing more damage and having more effects.
    * enemies’ items drop with some probability, not all the time—perhaps 20% for each item.
* since items have a level, they become obsolete as the character themselves grows in level. this creates a nice dynamic where the character is constantly looking for new, better equipment.
* Reach goal: we should allow the player to take custom actions by typing “/[action they want to take]”. the, a difficulty class will be set based on how likely the difficulty class agent thinks this action is to succeed, and the agent will roll and add its ability score and compare it against the ability class to determine if it succeeds. if it does, the difficulty class agent resolves the effect.

## optimisation (only implement once other things are working, or if speed becomes an issue)
* the physics simulation should not wait for the character or orchestrator LLMs to produce output. those agents should be run asynchronously, so they wait in the game instead of making the game lag.
* once normal actions are working, look into this extension: in the LLM calls that are run while an action is being performed, output whether or not to continue this action as well as the planned next action. when the action is done, immediately start performing the planned next action as you start computing the actual next action given the updated observation. when the new action is done computing, if it matches the predicted action that started, then perfect. if not, then stop the current action and start the other action.
* if the world gets laggy due to terrain generation, create a spatial index of the terrain and unload it when the character leaves, and reload it when the character comes back.
* at distances farther away from the nearest player character, we should back off the llm calls for speed reasons. of course, the frequency of the action recalculation should be lowered, but this is only relevant for actions that take a long time. most actions will be quick. so, we should also institute a growing 

# ability checks
* since ability checks are one-off and should be based on hooks instead of happening consistently like the orchestrator, there should be a separate LLM that gets called to resolve the ability checks. this will be the difficulty class agent.
* the agent decides the difficulty class based on how likely it thinks the action is to succeed. it also decides the relevant ability score to use in the comparison (INT, STR, WIS, etc).
* once it has decided the DC and ability score class, a roll is made to determine if the check actually succeeds.
* if it does, then another LLM call is made to resolve the effect, kind of like the orchestrator (we group this into the difficulty class agent, but it’s a different system prompt)

## terrain generation
* use procedural world generation. work hard to make this right—once an initial version is implemented, test it out by generating the world in different regions, taking screenshots, and analysing those screenshots to make it better.
* a good way to do world generation is to create tilemaps, and maintain probabilities (or functions that output probabilities) for each tile. for example, we can say that there is a 10% chance of a tree spawning on top of a grass tile, but a 50% chance if there is another tree within 3 tiles. or, we can say that the probability of spawning a non-path tile on the end of a path tile is 0%, because the path tile has to continue to another path tile or have a path end tile (this is just an example). these probabilities and probability functions can be tuned to make the terrain generation better.

## multiplayer
* this is a multiplayer game. make sure to allow multiple player characters in the world. in the first version, it should just be local multiplayer, but during implementation keep in mind that we will eventually want to implement online multiplayer.

## libraries
* for the game engine, i recommend using ursina, but if there is a better alternative you can use it.
* for the llm calls, we should provide an option: either use openrouter, or run the llm locally. locally, you can use ollama or any alternative if there is a better one.

## assets
* the sproutlands assets should be used for the majority of the terrain
* the dungeontileset assets should be used for dungeons
* the minifolks asset packs should be used for the characters.
* the rpgitempack assets should be used for the items.
